steps.dialogue: Welcome back! In this lesson, we're going to build on what you learned last time about unit fractions.

[event:6966a3e9c1c2f000] Last time you learned how to name unit fractions like [event:68fba12429b3e48b] one-third and one-fourth. That's an important skill, because unit fractions always show exactly [event:695ed79dd518f8a6] ONE part of a whole. Today we'll build on that knowledge, but first, let's make sure you remember those unit fractions clearly.

See how each bar on your screen has just [event:68fba35cd4b3091d]  one part shaded? These are the unit fractions you know. We'll do a quick review, and then we'll explore what happens when we want to show fractions that are MORE [event:68fba3b101ae16a8] than one part.
[event:6966a4880f891a75]Take a look at the bars here. Click on the bar that shows [fraction numerator=1, denominator=3]one third[/fraction]. Remember, that means 1 part shaded out of 3 total parts.

prompt.text: Click the bar that shows [fraction numerator=1, denominator=3]one third[/fraction].

step.dialogue: [event:696016b6d36c91cb]Not quite, try counting the total parts in each bar.
[event:696016b6d36c91cb]Let's count together, watch as each part lights up: [event: interaction_1_md_1]Which bar is the one with three total parts?

[event:696016b6d36c91cb]Let me help you. The top bar has [event:69601b9cd1f3719c] 3 equal parts total with 1 shaded. That makes [event:697cecdc0384a17c] [fraction numerator=1, denominator=3]one third[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: You found it! [event: interaction_1_cor]One out of three equal parts makes [fraction numerator=1, denominator=3]one third[/fraction].

steps.dialogue: [event:68fba65589971447]A unit fraction always shows [event: interaction_1_con] one equal part of a whole. Notice how the top number is always 1 in unit fractions. That never changes.
Here's something interesting, even though they all show one part, not all unit fractions are the same size.[event:68fba5440d16ca60] Let's see what determines how big or small that one part is.

metadata.notes: TTS out of date on correct answer -Sam

steps.dialogue: [event:6966a4e9d46875e6] Now look at these two bars. Which piece is bigger, [fraction numerator=1 denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction] or [fraction numerator=1 denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction]?

prompt.text: Click the bar with the bigger piece.

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let's try again. Take another look at the size of each shaded piece.

[event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let me help you compare. See how the [event:697cf1c9c4423055][fraction numerator=1, denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction] piece is bigger than [event:697cf1d2c95be50e] the [fraction numerator=1, denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction] piece? Fewer parts means bigger pieces.

[event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start] [event:6961712b59656142] Watch how I compare: The bar with 4 parts has bigger pieces because there are fewer parts. The bar with 6 parts has smaller pieces. So [fraction numerator=1, denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction] is bigger than [fraction numerator=1, denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Right. When we have fewer parts, each part is bigger. [event:697cef244df79e9f][fraction numerator=1 denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction] takes up more space[event:697cef3603aa1067] than one-sixth because the whole [event:697cef70463d3b3e]is split into fewer pieces.

steps.dialogue: Good! You remember unit fractions, they always show just one part.
But what if you want to show[event:68fba6d43f61b6ce] two parts? Or three [event:68fba6f68f2f25eb]parts? Let's find out.
